title: Colloidal layer deposition with a controllable number of layers and compositional order
authors: Akshaya Kumar Jena, Aashima Aashima, Pritam Kumar Jana, Bortolo Matteo Mognetti
abstract: We design a system with a binary suspension of colloids and a surface that triggers the self-assembly of crystallites with a finite thickness. The proposed design allows controlling the number of layers forming the aggregate and constrains the two types of particles to lie on different planes. These functionalities are achieved by decorating the colloids and the surface with multiple DNA oligomers featuring specific interactions. The surface triggers a chain of reactions between DNA oligomers, leading to localized self-assembly. Equilibrium principles control the thickness of the aggregates. Instead, compositional order is achieved by engineering the reaction kinetics between DNA oligomers in a way that limits interactions between colloids of the same type. We validate our design using theory and reaction-diffusion simulation algorithms, which capture the multibody nature of the interactions. This work demonstrates how engineering the kinetics provides a new avenue for controlling the morphology of aggregates assembled by DNA.
summary: The paper presents a method to control the thickness and composition of colloidal layers by engineering the kinetics of DNA strand-displacement reactions. By using reaction kinetics to suppress unwanted equilibrium interactions, the researchers achieved a system that assembles into predictable, alternating layers. This provides a new way to program the morphology of complex soft-matter aggregates.
main_problem: Controlling the morphology, thickness, and compositional order of DNA-mediated colloidal aggregates by overcoming kinetic limitations and undesired equilibrium interactions.
main_result: Demonstrated that engineering reaction kinetics via toehold-mediated strand displacement can act as a digital filter to suppress undesired interactions, enabling the assembly of multi-layered crystals with alternating particle types.
method: A hybrid simulation approach combining Brownian dynamics for particle positions and the Gillespie algorithm for reaction-diffusion dynamics, validated against theoretical free-energy calculations.
model_or_system: A binary suspension of self-protected colloids (Types A and B) and a functionalized surface, where DNA oligomers facilitate reversible formation of two-strand and three-strand complexes.
key_observables: Number of layers in the aggregate, compositional order, number of DNA complexes (loops, bridges), and effective particle-surface/particle-particle interaction potentials.
important_parameters: Number of ligands per particle (N_L), chemical potential (mu), reaction timescale (tau_0), and hybridization free energies (delta G).
main_assumptions: Neglects non-specific ligand-ligand interactions, assumes ligands are thin rods, and assumes lateral diffusion of tethered linkers is much faster than the self-assembly timescale.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates the binary system and DNA design; Figure 2 compares kinetic vs. equilibrium interaction potentials; Figure 3 shows steady-state layer configurations and scaling with N_L and density; Figure 4 tracks complex dynamics over time.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of kinetic limitations in DNA-assembly, presents a design for controlled layer deposition, derives the mathematical forces and free energy of the system, and uses reaction-diffusion simulations to validate the kinetic control mechanism.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: The model. (a) Illustration of a binary system comprising colloids (A and B) functionalized with two pairs of ligands (a1, a2 and b1, b2) in the presence of a surface functionalized with a single type of ligand (b1). (b) Schematic representation of ligands, consisting of a hydrophobic tag (anchoring the DNA to supported lipid bilayers), a rigid double-stranded DNA spacer, and a sticky end composed of three modules. (c) List of all possible complexes in the system, including loops and bridges that cross-link particles and link particles to the surface. Complexes that can be kinetically suppressed are highlighted in gray. For clarity, particles are represented as planes. (d)...

Figure 2: Kinetic vs Equilibrium Interactions. Effective, kinetic interactions (black circles) and equilibrium interactions (red squares) for a single particle near the surface (a, b), and a particle near another A-type particle bound to the surface (c, d). A-type particles bind the surface (a) but do not bind surface-bound A particles (c). Instead, B-type particles bind surface-bound A particles (d) and are repelled by the surface (b). Equilibrium calculations are detailed in SI sec. E.60 In all cases, equilibrium interactions are attractive. We used NL = 50, ρS = 2 L−2, β∆G0 = −10, and β∆GT = −2.

Figure 3: Colloidal layer deposition with a controllable number of layers and com- positional order. Multi-particle reaction-diffusion simulations result in the self-assembly of crystals made of a controllable number of stacked, same-type colloid planes. The number of planes is controlled by NL (NL = 50, 75, and 100 in a, b, and c) and the chemical potential (ρid = 10−7, 10−6, and 10−4 in d, e, and f). In the top and bottom row we set ρid = 10−5 and NL = 75, respectively. In all panels we set ρS = 0.28 L−2, β∆G0 = −20, and β∆GT = −2.
